source: Investopedia, CFPB, SEC, Vanguard Education, Morningstar

compound_interest.name: Compound Interest
compound_interest.oneLiner: Earning interest on your interest — the snowball effect of money.
compound_interest.explanation: When you invest $1,000 at 7% annual return, you earn $70 the first year. But the second year, you earn 7% on $1,070 = $74.90. The third year, 7% on $1,144.90 = $80.14. Each year, the growth accelerates because you're earning returns on your previous returns. Over 30 years, $1,000 becomes $7,612 — without adding another dollar.

keyNumbers.ruleOf72: Divide 72 by your return rate to estimate years to double. 72/7 = ~10 years to double at 7%.

index_fund.name: Index Fund
index_fund.oneLiner: A fund that buys every stock in a market index, like the S&P 500.
index_fund.explanation: Instead of picking individual stocks, an index fund buys ALL of them in proportion. An S&P 500 index fund owns 500 companies. A total market fund owns 3,700+. Because nobody has to research and pick stocks, fees are incredibly low (0.03% vs 1%+ for actively managed funds). Over 20 years, 90%+ of actively managed funds LOSE to index funds. This is why Warren Buffett recommends them for most people.

keyNumbers.avgExpenseRatio: 0.03-0.10% for index funds vs 0.50-1.50% for active
keyNumbers.feeDifference: On $100K invested for 30 years: 0.03% fees = $568K. 1% fees = $432K. That's $136K lost to fees.

emergency_fund.name: Emergency Fund
emergency_fund.oneLiner: 3-6 months of living expenses in a savings account for unexpected events.
emergency_fund.explanation: Job loss, medical emergency, car breakdown — life happens. Without an emergency fund, these events go on credit cards at 20%+ interest, creating a debt spiral. The fund should be in a high-yield savings account (not invested in stocks) because you need to access it quickly and can't afford it to be down 20% when you need it.

dollar_cost_averaging.name: Dollar-Cost Averaging
dollar_cost_averaging.oneLiner: Investing the same amount regularly regardless of market conditions.
dollar_cost_averaging.explanation: Instead of trying to time the market (impossible), you invest $500 every month no matter what. When prices are high, you buy fewer shares. When prices are low, you buy more. Over time, this averages out to a good price and removes emotion from investing. Your 401k already does this automatically with every paycheck.

asset_allocation.name: Asset Allocation
asset_allocation.oneLiner: How you split your money between stocks, bonds, and cash.
asset_allocation.explanation: The single most important investment decision is not WHICH stocks to buy — it's how much to put in stocks vs bonds vs cash. Stocks grow faster but are volatile. Bonds are stable but grow slowly. The classic rule: subtract your age from 110 for your stock percentage. Age 30 = 80% stocks, 20% bonds. Age 60 = 50/50. This automatically becomes more conservative as you age.

tax_advantaged_accounts.name: Tax-Advantaged Accounts (401k, IRA, Roth)
tax_advantaged_accounts.oneLiner: Accounts that reduce your taxes now or in retirement.
tax_advantaged_accounts.explanation: Traditional 401k/IRA: money goes in pre-tax (reduces your taxable income NOW), grows tax-free, taxed when you withdraw in retirement. Roth IRA: money goes in after-tax (no tax break now), grows tax-free, and withdrawals are TAX-FREE in retirement. The rule of thumb: if you expect to be in a higher tax bracket in retirement, Roth is better. If lower, Traditional is better. Most young people should prioritize Roth.

keyNumbers.employerMatch: Average employer matches 50-100% of first 3-6% of salary. Not contributing enough to get the full match is leaving free money on the table.

debt_avalanche_vs_snowball.name: Debt Avalanche vs Snowball
debt_avalanche_vs_snowball.oneLiner: Two strategies for paying off multiple debts.
debt_avalanche_vs_snowball.explanation: Avalanche: pay minimums on all debts, put extra money toward the HIGHEST interest rate first. Mathematically optimal — saves the most money. Snowball: pay minimums on all debts, put extra money toward the SMALLEST balance first. Psychologically powerful — quick wins build momentum. The best method is whichever one you'll actually stick with.

expense_ratio.name: Expense Ratio
expense_ratio.oneLiner: The annual fee a fund charges, expressed as a percentage of your investment.
expense_ratio.explanation: A 1% expense ratio on $100,000 means you pay $1,000/year in fees — whether the fund gains or loses money. This sounds small but compounds devastatingly over decades. The difference between 0.03% (Vanguard index fund) and 1% (typical actively managed fund) on $100K over 30 years at 7% return is over $130,000 in lost wealth. Always check the expense ratio.

diversification.name: Diversification
diversification.oneLiner: Don't put all your eggs in one basket.
diversification.explanation: Owning one stock is gambling. Owning 500 stocks is investing. When one company fails, the others compensate. This is why index funds are powerful — VTI gives you 3,700+ companies in one purchase. True diversification also means owning international stocks (VXUS), bonds (BND), and having different account types (401k, Roth, taxable).

inflation.name: Inflation
inflation.oneLiner: Your money loses purchasing power over time.
inflation.explanation: At 3% inflation, something that costs $100 today will cost $134 in 10 years and $181 in 20 years. This is why keeping money in a checking account (0% return) actually LOSES value. Your investments need to earn MORE than inflation to build real wealth. The stock market's ~10% historical return minus ~3% inflation = ~7% real return.

credit_score.name: Credit Score
credit_score.oneLiner: A number (300-850) that represents how reliable you are with borrowed money.
credit_score.explanation: Your score affects interest rates on mortgages, car loans, credit cards, and even apartment applications. The difference between a 680 and 760 credit score on a $300,000 mortgage can be $100+/month in higher interest. Factors: payment history (35%), amounts owed (30%), length of credit history (15%), new credit (10%), credit mix (10%). The fastest way to improve: pay on time, keep credit utilization under 30%.
